title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 22 - ‘Children of Men’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins put Alfonso Cuarón's Children of Men at No. 22, a pick Sean kept threatening to drop before both of them rewatched it and decided they'd ranked it too low. They go through the single-take set pieces, the immaculate-conception reading, the King Crimson and Radiohead needle drops, and the 2006 Oscars that handed the film three technical nominations and no wins while The Departed took Best Picture.

films.title: Children of Men
films.note: The pick. Alfonso Cuarón directs and shares a writing credit with Timothy J. Sexton, David Arata, Mark Fergus and Hawk Ostby, adapting P.D. James's 1992 novel; Clive Owen, Claire-Hope Ashitey, Julianne Moore, Chiwetel Ejiofor, Pam Ferris, Charlie Hunnam and Michael Caine star, shot by Emmanuel Lubezki. Set in a 2027 London after 18-plus years of worldwide infertility, it follows Theo, a burned-out former activist pulled back in by his ex, Julian, to escort Kee, a pregnant refugee, to safety. Sean and Amanda hadn't rewatched it before longlisting it, bumped it down the schedule for Something's Gotta Give, then both watched it and thought it might belong in the top 10. They read it as a perfect screenplay married to athletic filmmaking: the single-shot car ambush, the closing camp raid Theo runs through following the baby's cries, absolutely grotesque things staged in the background of nearly every frame. Cuarón added the refugee and camp material (Abu Ghraib and Guantánamo imagery, cages, ICE-like separations) to a James novel that was more class-focused; a flu pandemic that killed Theo and Julian's son prefigures COVID. Slavoj Žižek's reading of the conception as figuratively immaculate (no sex in the film) comes up, as does the Danny Huston scene, where a powerful man hoards Guernica and Michelangelo's David while the world ends outside.

films.title: Something's Gotta Give
films.note: The list entry the hosts bumped up while Sean stalled on Children of Men. Diane Keaton was Oscar-nominated for it and lost; a prior pick on their 25-for-25 list.

films.title: Contagion
films.note: Amanda's note in their doc pairs it with Children of Men as the other film whose pandemic dread landed as prescient; Sean recalls it as ill-timed viewing in early 2020.

films.title: Y Tu Mamá También
films.note: The Cuarón road movie he calls his first real film; Sean says he seriously considered pushing for it in this spot, and its handheld, follow-the-characters style is a direct line to Children of Men.

films.title: Sólo con tu Pareja
films.note: Cuarón's debut, made in Mexico; cited to show how different his early films are from one another.

films.title: A Little Princess
films.note: Cuarón's American film; Amanda tells Sean not to fast-forward past it, calling it a good movie.

films.title: Great Expectations
films.note: Cuarón's much-touted Dickens adaptation with Gwyneth Paltrow and Ethan Hawke that fizzled, the failure that pushed him to reset with Y Tu Mamá También.

films.title: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
films.note: The paycheck franchise job Cuarón took before Children of Men; the hosts rate it one of the best franchise movies of the century.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Cuarón's personal Mexico City memoir and the last film he made before a seven-year gap; the point after which he stopped being a regular topic on the show.

films.title: Gravity
films.note: The Cuarón crowd-pleaser the Oscars did reward, which Sean reads as a make-good; he loved it in theaters but finds it doesn't hold up, thin on character and only 90 minutes.

films.title: Closer
films.note: The Mike Nichols film from Clive Owen's brief run as a top-billed star.

films.title: Croupier
films.note: The breakout that set up Owen's early-2000s stretch of cool movies.

films.title: Wimbledon
films.note: Sean credits Owen with a very important intro voiceover; Amanda doesn't remember it.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: The Best Picture winner of 2006, held up as the year that beat Children of Men; the hosts' number-one guy, with all respect.

films.title: Babel
films.note: A Best Picture nominee that year; Sean flatly says it sucks, and notes Iñárritu is one of Cuarón's Three Amigos alongside Guillermo del Toro.

films.title: Letters from Iwo Jima
films.note: Clint Eastwood's late-December 2006 release and a Best Picture nominee, one of the films that crowded Children of Men out.

films.title: Little Miss Sunshine
films.note: The Sundance phenomenon and box-office hit that ran the table that awards season; a Best Picture nominee.

films.title: The Queen
films.note: A Best Picture nominee Amanda likes a lot, but, both agree, not Children of Men.

films.title: United 93
films.note: Paul Greengrass's docudrama, which Sean singles out as a genuinely great directing nominee that year.

films.title: Michael Clayton
films.note: A prior 25-for-25 entry, and the only Oscar win on the list so far: Tilda Swinton, Best Supporting Actress.

films.title: The Handmaiden
films.note: A prior list entry, which the hosts note wasn't even nominated for Best International Feature.

films.title: Z
films.note: Costa-Gavras's most famous political drama, first of the if-you-liked-this recommendations.

films.title: Missing
films.note: The Costa-Gavras film with Jack Lemmon and Sissy Spacek the hosts covered on their 1982 movie draft with Tracy Letts.

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: Recommended as the other prescient police-state movie, its smog and dystopia set in a 2019 the film imagined from 1982.

films.title: 12 Monkeys
films.note: An if-you-liked-this recommendation, wildly different in tone but similar in its societal-collapse register.

films.title: Punishment Park
films.note: Peter Watkins's 1970s British film, a deeper-cut recommendation, different in style but not in tone.

films.title: Civil War
films.note: An if-you-liked-this pick: another end-of-the-world group movie whose action and world-building one host rates highly.

Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban.title: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
